title: TodoItem status field is String not enum — invalid states unrepresentable rule violated
description:
## Severity
Major

## Dimension
Type Safety / SOLID-L (LSP) / Naming

## Location
src/tools/todo.rs:9-15 (TodoItem struct) and 100-116 (status validation)

## Violation
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct TodoItem {
    pub content: String,
    pub status: String,   // <-- stringly-typed
    #[serde(rename = "activeForm")]
    pub active_form: String,
}
```

The status field is `String` and validated against the literal slice `["pending", "in_progress", "completed"]` (line 102) at write time. Every downstream consumer must remember to re-check the value:
- todo.rs:175-180 (string-compare against "completed" and "in_progress")
- todo.rs:213-217 (match on str)
- get_todo_list() returns TodoItem with status as String — every external caller must hardcode the magic strings again.

CLAUDE.md explicitly says "Use newtype pattern for domain types... Prefer enums over stringly-typed code... Leverage the type system to make invalid states unrepresentable" (Type Safety section). This struct is the textbook violation. The validation message "Must be: pending, in_progress, completed" is even constructed from the same hardcoded list — drift between the struct, the validator, and the consumers is inevitable.

## Mandated refactor
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq, Eq)]
#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum TodoStatus { Pending, InProgress, Completed }

pub struct TodoItem {
    pub content: String,
    pub status: TodoStatus,
    pub active_form: String,
}
```

`serde` handles validation; every downstream `match todo.status { Pending => ..., }` is exhaustive and the compiler catches new variants. The hand-rolled status_icon match in todo_read becomes infallible — no `"[?]" => _` arm needed.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
